slides.title: 1. Hydrostatic Skeleton
slides.description:
Found in soft-bodied animals like Earthworms and Slugs.

How it works: The body is filled with fluid (liquid). The muscles squeeze against this fluid to create movement.

Exam Example: An earthworm moves using circular and longitudinal muscles pushing against its fluid-filled body.

slides.title: 2. Exoskeleton
slides.description:
Found in Insects (like cockroaches), Crabs, and Spiders.

Definition: A hard outer covering made of a substance called Chitin.
Molting: Since the exoskeleton does not grow, the insect must shed it off to grow larger. This process is called Molting (Ecdysis).

slides.title: 3. Endoskeleton
slides.description:
Found in Vertebrates (Humans, Cows, Fish, Birds).

Definition: A skeleton found inside the body, made of bone and cartilage.

Advantage: It grows with the animal, so no molting is required. It provides a strong framework for large muscles.
